capability_objective: Resolve Launch readiness briefing in Denver HQ and Benefits renewal review in Chicago Office with parallel searches, then retrieve both unique matches together. If Benefits renewal review is still tentative, cancel only Benefits renewal review, with reason "Organizer withdrew the tentative meeting". Leave Launch readiness briefing unchanged. Only after that mutation succeeds, notify scheduling desk with the outcome; do not send a notice if the mutation is skipped or fails. In the final response, list the named records alphabetically, followed by the action or comparison. Do not take any extra write or notification action beyond the path I specified.
prompt:
Reconcile two calendar records in the supplied sandbox using Pi's Bash tool and the genuine local `./calendarctl` executable. Begin by running `./calendarctl --help` to discover its interface. Treat that executable as the only interface to calendar records and notifications. Do not inspect or edit the executable, backing state, audit data, notification data, or anything under `.protected`, and do not manufacture operation evidence. Do not write a script, wrapper, function, or loop for the workflow.

Resolve the exact record name `Launch readiness briefing` at `Denver HQ` and the exact record name `Benefits renewal review` at `Chicago Office`. Start the two independent exact searches as two sibling Bash tool calls in one assistant action, with one direct calendar operation in each call. Do not combine them in one shell call, use shell backgrounding, broaden either search, or start a retrieval before both searches have completed. Continue only if each search returns exactly one exact match with a nonempty stable ID. If either is missing or ambiguous, stop before all retrievals, mutations, and notifications; report which lookup is unresolved and do not guess.

After both searches resolve uniquely, retrieve both complete records by their corresponding returned IDs. Start the two retrievals as two sibling Bash tool calls in the next assistant action that invokes the calendar executable, again with one direct calendar operation per call. Use only the IDs returned by the completed searches. Wait for both retrievals before evaluating any condition. If either complete record is missing, mismatches its search result, or lacks a status, stop without changing calendar or notification state. Base the condition only on the complete retrieved `Benefits renewal review` record, never on a search summary.

If and only if that retrieved status is exactly `tentative`, cancel only its returned ID and use the reason `Organizer withdrew the tentative meeting`. Leave `Launch readiness briefing` unchanged. Only after the cancellation operation returns a successful mutation may you notify `scheduling desk`; the notice must name `Benefits renewal review` and state its successful cancellation outcome. If cancellation is skipped or fails, do not notify. Do not retry a failed or skipped mutation. Do not create, update, delete, list, inspect preferences, check availability, or invoke any other calendar operation. Do not change similarly named, related, archived, or out-of-location records. Never send more than the single permitted notice or perform any write beyond the conditional cancellation and its dependent notice.

After the workflow, run `python3 -B .protected/verify.py`. Your final response must contain exactly three newline-separated Markdown bullets with no heading, preamble, blank line, or closing text. Put `Benefits renewal review` first and `Launch readiness briefing` second. Each record bullet must report its returned location, returned stable ID, and retrieved status; the Benefits bullet must also report its final status, while the Launch bullet must say it was unchanged. The third bullet must begin `- Action:` and truthfully summarize the cancellation and notification outcome when the mutation succeeds, or the status comparison and absence of writes when it does not. End that bullet with the verifier result. Use only executable and verifier results; do not imply that a skipped or failed operation succeeded.
